title: How to Use Intuition When Making Parenting Decisions
meta_description: Meta Description: Trust your gut in parenting. That tight chest isn't anxiety. It's intuition guiding you past what logic misses. Learn to use it.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Parenting Decisions
og_description: Meta Description: Trust your gut in parenting. That tight chest isn't anxiety. It's intuition guiding you past what logic misses. Learn to use it.
body:
You're Not Overthinking It. You're Sensing Something They Can't See.

You've been there. Standing in the middle of Target at 4:15 PM, your kid is melting down over a snack you don't have, and you feel a tightness in your chest that has nothing to do with caffeine. Your brain is screaming "just get the crackers, it's fine," but something else - something deeper - is whispering a quiet, insistent "no." And you don't know why.

That's not anxiety. That's not overparenting. That's your intuition - your nervous system's way of telling you that a decision needs more time, more information, or a completely different direction. And in the chaos of parenting, that voice is the most underused tool you've got.

I'm Kalesh. I work with people who feel things deeply - empaths, highly sensitive parents, folks who've been told their whole lives that they're "too much." And the number one thing I hear is this: "I don't trust myself when it comes to my kids." You've been conditioned to override your gut with logic, with what the books say, with what other parents do. But your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. That's not a glitch. That's your compass.

Let's talk about how to actually use it when the stakes feel high - and when they feel small. Because intuition isn't just for big, dramatic moments. It's for the Tuesday afternoon decisions that shape your kid's sense of safety and your own sense of sanity.

"Your intuition isn't a mystical voice from the heavens. It's your body's accumulated wisdom, speaking in the only language it knows - sensation, impulse, and a quiet knowing that doesn't need to argue its case."

Why Parenting Short-Circuits Your Intuition (And What to Do About It)

Here's the thing about being a parent: you're constantly being told what to do. From the moment you announce you're expecting, the advice comes in waves. Sleep when the baby sleeps. Don't let them cry it out. Actually, do let them cry it out. Breast is best. Fed is best. Screen time is poison. Screen time is fine if it's educational.

You don't even have time to check in with yourself before the next opinion hits. And after a while, you start to believe that everyone else knows better than you do. Right?!

But here's what happens when you outsource your decisions to external authorities: your intuition gets quieter. Not because it stops talking, but because you stop listening. Your body still sends signals - a knot in your stomach, a tightness in your jaw, a sudden urge to change the plan - but you've been trained to dismiss them as "overthinking" or "being dramatic."

The truth is, your intuition is built on pattern recognition. Your brain has been collecting data since you were born - about safety, about connection, about what feels aligned. When you get a gut feeling about a decision, it's not random. It's your system saying, "I've seen this pattern before. Pay attention."

So the first step is simple: stop apologizing for having a feeling. You don't need to justify it. You don't need to explain it. You just need to honor it long enough to let it speak.

The Three Layers of Intuitive Decision-Making (And Why Most Parents Only Use the First One)

Most of us think intuition is a single thing - a "knowing" that either shows up or doesn't. But it's actually three distinct layers, and each one serves a different purpose in parenting.

Layer One: The Gut Punch

This is the most obvious layer. It's the sudden, physical sensation that something is off. Your stomach clenches. Your shoulders tighten. You feel a wave of unease that has no obvious source. This layer is designed for immediate safety. If your toddler is about to touch a hot stove, you don't need to think - you just move. Right?!

But this layer also shows up in quieter ways. Like when you're about to say "yes" to a playdate and your gut says "no" for no apparent reason. Or when you're considering a daycare and you walk in and feel a heaviness you can't explain. Honor that. It's not being dramatic. It's your system reading the room faster than your conscious mind can process.

Layer Two: The Slow Knowing

This one is trickier because it doesn't feel urgent. It's a quiet, persistent thought that keeps circling back. You might be making dinner, and the idea of having "the talk" with your teenager comes to mind. You push it away. It comes back. You push it away again. It keeps coming.

This layer isn't loud. It's patient. It's the voice that says, "You know what you need to do. You're just avoiding it." And in parenting, this is where most of your important decisions live - the ones that don't have a deadline but have a cost if you ignore them.

Layer Three: The Body Read

This is the most subtle layer, and the one most parents overlook. It's the practice of checking in with your body before, during, and after a decision. When you're considering a choice, take a breath and scan your body. Where do you feel tension? Where do you feel ease? Does your chest feel open or closed? Does your breathing feel shallow or full?

Your body is giving you feedback in real time. The trick is learning to read it without judgment. If you feel tight when you think about saying "yes" to something, that's data. If you feel a sense of expansion when you think about saying "no," that's also data. Neither is right or wrong. It's just information.

One of the best tools I've found for tuning into this layer is a simple journaling practice. I recommend The Intuitive Parent: A Guide to Trusting Your Instincts in Raising a Child - it walks you through exactly how to distinguish between fear-based reactions and genuine intuitive hits. See on Amazon (paid link)

The One Question That Cuts Through the Noise

I'm going to give you a question that has saved my sanity more times than I can count. Next time you're stuck between two options - should I let them quit the sport? Should I say yes to the sleepover? Should I enforce the screen time limit or let it slide? - ask yourself this:

"If I made this decision, and no one else ever knew about it, would I still feel peaceful about it?"

This question bypasses the noise of other people's opinions. It bypasses the guilt. It bypasses the "what will they think" spiral. It gets straight to your own alignment. If the answer is yes, you have your answer. If the answer is no - or if there's a knot in your stomach - keep digging.

This works because most of our parenting anxiety comes from imagined judgment. We're afraid of being seen as too strict, too permissive, too weird, too anxious. But when you remove the audience, the real truth surfaces. And that truth is almost always kinder and more grounded than the noise in your head.

When Your Kid's Intuition Conflicts With Yours (And How to Handle It)

This is a big one, and it doesn't get talked about enough. Your kid has their own intuitive system, and it's just as valid as yours. Sometimes they'll say "I don't want to go to Grandma's house" and you'll feel a pang in your gut that says the same thing. But sometimes they'll say "I don't want to go to soccer practice" and your intuition says "they actually need to go."

So how do you know whose intuition to follow?

Start by checking your own body first. If your kid says they don't want to do something, pause. Take a breath. Scan your system. Does their refusal trigger a protective response in you? Or does it trigger a sense of recognition - like "yeah, I felt that too"?

If it's protective - like you're worried they'll miss out or look bad - that's probably your own stuff. In that case, your job is to get curious. Ask them: "What feels off about it? Can you tell me more?" Don't interrogate. Just listen.

If it's recognition - like you feel a quiet "yeah, that's not right" in your own body - then honor it. You don't have to explain it to anyone. You can just say, "You know what? I think you're right. Let's skip it."

This is where trust gets built - not just between you and your kid, but between you and your own knowing. Every time you honor a gut feeling, you strengthen the signal for next time.

Practical Tools for the Trenches (When You Don't Have Time to Meditate)

Let's be real. You don't have time for a 20-minute visualization before every decision. You need tools that work in the middle of a meltdown, in the carpool line, at 11 PM when you're exhausted and your teenager is asking for something you're not sure about.

Here are three that actually work:

1. The Three-Breath Pause

Before you answer a question or make a decision, take three conscious breaths. That's it. Not a full meditation. Just three breaths where you feel the air move in and out of your body. On the third exhale, notice what your first instinct is. That instinct - the one that shows up before your brain starts spinning - is your intuition. Capture it before your inner critic gets a word in.

2. The "Yes/No" Body Scan

When you're torn between two options, say each one out loud. "Yes to the playdate." Pause. Scan your body. "No to the playdate." Pause. Scan your body. One of them will feel different - lighter, more open, more settled. That's your answer. It doesn't have to make logical sense. It just has to feel true in your body.

3. The Post-Decision Check-In

After you make a decision - especially a hard one - check in with yourself 24 hours later. How does your body feel? Do you feel a sense of relief, even if the decision was hard? Or do you feel a lingering tightness? This isn't about second-guessing yourself. It's about gathering data for next time. Over time, you'll start to recognize the signature of a good decision in your body.

If you want to go deeper into this practice, I highly recommend Raising Your Spirited Child: A Guide for Parents Whose Child Is More Intense, Sensitive, Perceptive, Persistent, and Energetic. It's not a traditional intuition book, but it's one of the best resources I know for understanding the nervous system of a sensitive child - which is exactly what you're working with when you're tuning into your own intuitive signals. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Myth of the "Right" Decision (And What to Aim For Instead)

Here's the thing that nobody tells you: there is no single "right" decision. Parenting doesn't work that way. You can make a choice that feels perfect in the moment and have it blow up in your face. You can make a choice that feels terrible and have it turn out beautifully. That's not because your intuition was wrong. It's because life is complex, and you're not a fortune teller.

So stop aiming for "right." Aim for "aligned."

An aligned decision is one that feels true to who you are and what you value, even if it's hard. It's a decision that you can look back on and say, "I made that choice from a place of love and clarity, not from fear or pressure." That's the goal. Not perfection. Not a guarantee of a good outcome. Just alignment.

And here's the beautiful thing about aligned decisions: even when they lead to hard outcomes, they don't leave you with regret. Because you know you did your best with the information you had. You trusted yourself. And that trust - not the outcome - is what builds your intuitive muscle over time.

One of the most grounding books I've found on this topic is The Conscious Parent: Transforming Ourselves, Empowering Our Children. It's not about getting it right. It's about staying present with what is, even when what is feels messy. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When You're Too Exhausted to Feel Anything

Let's be honest about this too. Some days you're so tired, so touched out, so overwhelmed that you can't feel your intuition at all. It's just static. And that's okay. You don't have to be "on" all the time. Your intuition isn't a muscle that gets stronger from constant use - it needs rest too.

On those days, the most intuitive thing you can do is surrender. Let the decision wait. Let someone else decide. Order takeout. Put on a movie. Lower the bar until it's on the floor. Your intuition will come back when you've had enough rest. It's not gone. It's just on standby.

And if you're in a season where you feel completely disconnected from your own knowing, start small. Don't try to make big parenting decisions. Just practice tuning in to what you want for breakfast. What you want to wear. What you want to watch. Rebuild the connection with your body in low-stakes ways, and the big stuff will follow.

For a gentle, practical guide to rebuilding that connection, I love The Gifts of Imperfection: Let Go of Who You Think You're Supposed to Be and Embrace Who You Are. It's not specifically about parenting, but it's about the courage to trust your own voice - which is exactly what you need when you're trying to parent from a place of alignment. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Bottom Line (And What I Want You to Walk Away With)

You are not broken. You are not overthinking. You are not too much. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's, and that includes the ability to sense things that aren't obvious. That's not a liability. That's your gift.

The next time you're standing in a decision - big or small - pause. Take a breath. Ask your body what it knows. And if the answer is quiet, don't panic. Just wait. It'll come. It always does.

You already know more than you think you do. The work is just learning to trust that knowing.

If you're ready to go deeper into this practice - to untangle the difference between fear and intuition, and to build a parenting life that actually feels like yours - I've put together a free assessment that walks you through exactly that. Take the Intuitive Parenting Assessment here and see where your trust is strong and where it needs some support.
